Follow planned landscaping designs to determine where to lay sod, sow grass, or plants and foliage. Lay sod. Haul or spread topsoil, and spread straw over seeded soil to hold soil in place. Rake, mulch, and compost leaves. Prune and trim trees, shrubs, and hedges, using shears, pruners, or chain saws. Workers’ tasks will be to prune and trim trees on the ground. Gather and remove litter. Maintain and repair tools, equipment using hand and power tools. Mix and spray or spread fertilizers, herbicides, or insecticides onto grass, shrubs, and trees, using hand or automatic sprayers or spreaders. Care for natural turf fields, making sure the underlying soil has the required composition to allow proper drainage and to support the grasses used on the fields. Complete maintenance on various ponds, lakes, and other stormwater control measures. Maintenance activities may include mowing, invasive plants, treatment or removal, trash removal turf establishment minor repairs, device inspection, etc. Operate tools and equipment in a safe and efficient manner as necessary to complete project scope includes but is not limited to zero-turn mowers, slope mowers, string trimmers, UTV, jon boat, kayak, handtools, etc. Communicate effectively with other crew members and Regional Manager to clarify project scope as needed and to avoid return visits to project sites. Inspect and maintain vehicles and equipment to ensure all tools are in safe, working condition at all times.

1) Use of fertilizers, herbicides, and insecticides
The employee is responsible for spraying aquatic-labeled Glyphosate near ponds and stormwater systems on invasive or nuisance vegetation. They are not responsible for mixing or deciding formulas; all mixtures are prepared and supervised by a commercial licensed pesticide applicator who is present during any pesticide application.
2) Operation of equipment including job boat
Employees use 10–12 ft. jon boats and kayaks. This position does not operate motorized boats; all boats are propelled manually with paddles.
3) Maintenance of ponds, lakes, and stormwater control measures
Maintenance includes mowing around the device, removal of invasive/nuisance vegetation, removal of accumulated sediment, trash removal, rearranging riprap to prevent erosion, and clearing other debris that may clog or damage the device. Tools and equipment used include lawn mowers, string trimmers, leaf blowers, and various hand tools.
4) Soil composition and turf fields
Although this position does not maintain turf fields, the employee performs similar work on vegetation areas. Tasks include:
•	Using aerators (which pull plugs out of the ground) and aeravators (which break up the soil) on Ventrac and walk-behind equipment.
•	Adding topsoil or compost to improve soil quality for growth and drainage.
•	Applying seed, fertilizer, and lime to promote healthy vegetation growth.
•	Work is typically performed on bare soils, slopes, and pond dams.
•	Locations include HOAs and commercial properties throughout the service area.
5) Maintenance on equipment
The employee maintains lawn mowers and small equipment such as leaf blowers and string trimmers. Maintenance tasks include checking tires, cleaning air filters, inspecting belts for damage, ensuring proper oil levels, and addressing other minor visible issues. Any major repairs are escalated for professional service.

Cook on the Grill: The grill cook’s primary task is preparing food on the grill. Grill cooks typically cook meat, but they may also be responsible for grilling other kinds of foods, like vegetables, depending on the restaurant’s menu. The grill cook monitors the meat’s temperature to ensure it is cooked safely.

- Plate Food: After the food is cooked, the grill cook is responsible for plating it and making it look good. Customers want their food to look appetizing, so the grill cook prepares food in an attractive manner.

- Prepare Ingredients: Before the food is cooked, grill cooks are responsible for preparing ingredients. This usually takes place before the restaurant opens and continues throughout the day as the grill cook uses ingredients.

- Clean the Grill: After the restaurant closes, and even throughout their shift, grill cooks are responsible for cleaning the grill. This involves scrubbing off remaining food and washing the general area. Cleanliness is of utmost importance in the kitchen, and the grill cook is responsible for making sure customers stay healthy.

- Train New Grill Cooks: When needed, experienced grill cooks may train new grill cooks when they are hired. This involves training them on how to use the kitchen equipment, prepare food, and cook food to healthy standards.

Commercial landscaping maintenance services include maintenance and upkeep of outdoor space through mowing, edging, trimming, and blowing outdoor features of a commercial or business spaces, public, or private open spaces to include schools or similar types of properties or multi-family properties. Commercial maintenance landscaping laborers will perform the following job duties; ground preparation for sod, lay sod, plant small trees and shrubs, plant plants and flowers in flowerbeds and other green space areas, fertilize sod, plants, and flowers using pre-mixed and bagged fertilizer to maintain healthy plants, trees, and turf, dig trenches for sprinkler lines, repair sprinkler lines, replacement of sprinkler heads, startup irrigation system, and maintain hardscape areas which is any of the non-living elements in landscape of a work site. Examples of hardscape are: crushed rock, mulch, sand, rocks, or boulders areas. Techniques include construction or installation involves the restoration or maintenance of non-living elements in outdoor spaces, such as non-plant and grass landscape areas, walkways with crushed rock or sand as base element, rock, or boulder areas. RFI response: Clarification of Demolition-Related Duties
With respect to the statement in Item F.a.2 that Construction Laborers “participate in demolitions” and “operate and care for construction equipment and machines,” the employer clarifies that Construction Laborers do not perform demolition work involving heavy machinery, powered demolition tools, or specialized equipment. All demolition activities that involve saw cutting, mechanical breaking, or removal of concrete or structures are performed exclusively by separately qualified equipment operators or skilled trades. Construction Laborers participate in demolition activities only in a supportive, manual labor capacity after mechanical demolition has been completed by others. Their tasks are limited to hand cleanup and site support, including collecting loose debris, removing small broken pieces of concrete or material left behind by equipment, sweeping and clearing work areas, and preparing surfaces for subsequent
construction activities. These duties are performed using basic hand tools such as shovels, brooms, rakes, and wheelbarrows. Construction Laborers do not operate construction equipment or machines such as saws, breakers, loaders, excavators, backhoes, or other powered or motorized equipment. They do not perform equipment maintenance beyond basic housekeeping, such as keeping work areas clear of debris. No specific make or model of equipment applies to Construction Laborers because they do not operate machinery. Their role is limited to general manual labor consistent with SOC 47-2061.

Clarification of Sewer, Water, and Storm Drainpipe-Related Duties
Regarding the statement in Item F.a.2 that Construction Laborers “install sewer, water, and storm drainpipes,” the employer clarifies that Construction Laborers do not perform pipe installation, alignment, connection, testing, or technical placement of utility systems. All pipe installation work is performed by licensed plumbers, pipefitters, or other skilled utility workers.
Construction Laborers assist these skilled workers only by performing general manual support tasks associated with utility installation projects. These tasks include carrying pipes and fittings to the work area, handing materials and tools to skilled workers, backfilling trenches after pipe installation has been completed by others, spreading bedding material as directed, and performing site cleanup. Construction Laborers do not connect pipes, seal joints, operate trenching equipment, or perform grading or technical installation work. The tools used by Construction Laborers for these tasks are limited to basic hand tools such as shovels, rakes, brooms, and wheelbarrows. They do not operate trenching machines, compactors, loaders, or other powered equipment, and no make or model of equipment applies to their role. Their duties remain strictly supportive and manual in nature, consistent with the Construction Laborer occupation under SOC 47-2061.

***RFI Response***

To clarify the existing duties, all installation‑related tasks referenced in Item F.a.2 are limited
to basic landscape labor functions that fall squarely within the scope of Standard Occupational
Classification (SOC) 37‑3011, Landscaping and Groundskeeping Workers. Workers do not
perform construction‑level carpentry, masonry, or equipment‑operator duties.

1. Installation of rock gardens, ponds, decks, drainage/irrigation systems, retaining walls,
fences, planters, and/or playground equipment: When these tasks arise, the workers’ role is
strictly limited to assisting senior staff with simple, manual labor such as placing small rocks,
positioning pre‑fabricated edging, setting lightweight planters, holding materials, digging
shallow holes, leveling soil, spreading mulch or gravel, and assembling pre‑made components.
Workers may use basic hand tools such as shovels, rakes, trowels, wheelbarrows, small
tampers, and simple battery‑powered hand tools (e.g., drill or screwdriver) for light fastening.
All structural construction, heavy equipment operation, cutting, framing, or specialty system
installation is performed by supervisors or subcontracted specialists.

2. Grading and contouring for proper drainage: Workers assist with light soil preparation only.
Tasks may include raking soil, smoothing surface areas, filling low spots by hand, lightly
tamping loose soil, and following supervisor instructions to create minor surface contours.
Materials consist of soil, sand, mulch, and small aggregate. Tools are limited to rakes, shovels,
hand tampers, and wheelbarrows. Workers do not perform machine grading or operate
grading equipment.

3. Installation of pavers: Workers assist with basic site preparation and placement of individual
pavers by hand. Tasks may include removing debris, leveling bedding sand with a rake, aligning
pavers, and lightly tapping them into place with a small hand mallet. Materials include sand,
gravel, and pre‑manufactured pavers. Tools include hand trowels, rakes, levels, knee pads,
and rubber mallets. Workers do not cut pavers, operate saws, or perform masonry.

Basic wage rate of pay from $13.77 per hour (regular shift) to $18.00 per hour (overnight shift). Workers may work at different basic wage rates of pay during the same work week based on where they are working. Tipped position for regular shift. Allowed tip credit applied per FLSA guidelines at $5.12 per hour. The cash wage paid will be $8.65-$13.77 per hour. In locations that exceed the hourly guaranteed basic wage rate of pay of $13.77 per hour, the employer will pay the difference and will not take a tip credit for any amount over $13.77 per hour. If in a given work week, the worker's tips do not meet the hourly tip credit, the employer will compensate for the difference between what is earned and the hourly guaranteed base rate for where the work was performed. All tips received by the tipped worker are retained by the worker except for a valid tip pooling arrangement limited to workers who customarily and regularly receive tips. The tip credit will not apply unless the worker has been notified of the employer's intention to exercise the tip credit. 

The employer may increase wage based on experience, changes in market conditions, and/or provide additional pay for performance and tenure.

Generally, when overtime is available it will be paid from $20.66 per hour to $27.00 per hour. The regular rate will be computed by adding together total earnings for the workweek from all sources, excluding tips received in excess of the tip credit, then dividing that number by the total hours worked in the workweek. If the regular rate exceeds $13.77 per hour, the employer will pay the difference between $13.77 per hour and the regular rate during an overtime week.

Título do cargo: Fast Food & Counter Workers – Mobile Food Concessions
Responsabilidades do cargo:
**RFI**
Issue 1.The food items this employer offers are prepackage & pre-made. Sausage, Hotdogs, Hamburgers & French Fries. The employees will need to grill & assemble hamburgers & hot dogs & cut & fry fries. They would also need to cut tomatoes, onions & lettuce for garnish. Tools & equipment: gas deep fryers; gas grills; food warmers, knives, tongs, potato cutter, can opener. All meats come prepackaged. The vegetables/toppings that are grilled with the items typically come fresh (green peppers, onions, jalapeno peppers). The fresh items will be washed (placed into a common colander or strainer) by running water over them in the sink, then placed onto a chopping board & cored/cut into the proper size pieces using a common knife & then placed into plastic food service containers pending being grilled). The meat items as well as the vegetable items would be placed on a grill to cook, using tongs to place them onto the grill & a turner spatula to move them around the grill. Olive oil would be poured onto the grill. Part of the grill would be at full heat to grill the meat items, part would be kept at medium heat to grill the vegetables, & parts of the grill would be kept at low temperature to hold the meats & vegetables (in separate areas) until they are ready to serve to the public. During the shift the worker would use a griddle scraper to scrape off any built up residue. At the end of the day the worker would use a pumice grill stone & a 50/50 mix or white distilled vinegar & water. The worker would pour prepackaged frozen fries into a fryer (similar to what someone would see at any other fast food establishment), when the timer goes off the basket raises itself automatically to drip the hot grease back into the fryer, then the worker when the second buzzer sounds, would pick up the basket with gloved hand, dump onto a tray, sprinkle with salt, & place into the appropriate container (medium, large or family sized). Continual cleaning of the work area during the shift & at the end of the day. Puttin supplies into refrigeration – or discarding. Are part of the duties. 
Issue 2. “setting up, tearing down” mobile food concessions. The employer would position the food concessions trailer in the assigned location at the event. During transit, metal awnings cover the glass windows. The worker would unlatch the locks, using their hands. The gas struts would raise the awnings to the correct height automatically. As items would shift during transit, the worker would carefully open the door, observe if everything was in the correct position, & if not, slide whatever was out of passion back into the correct location. Front counter, cash register, counter cloth/banner, trash cans, condiment, cutlery & napkin kiosk(s) would be unloaded by h& & placed into the correct locations. Each item is designed for travel & easy setup, with nothing weighing more than about 20 pounds.

***RFI Response***

In response to the RFI, we would like to confirm that the specific tasks of the Groundskeeper position related to "common areas of the resort" can be clarified as follows: 

The common areas of the resort include lawns, paths, gardens, sidewalks, deck areas, and patio areas. The tasks of the Groundskeeper position related to the above mentioned common areas of the resort include: lawn care (mowing, edging watering fertilizing and seeding) plant care (planting flowers, shrubs, trees; pruning, trimming, mulching weeding and seasonal cleanup, cleaning up fallen twigs, leaves, branches), debris removal (picking up trash, leaves, from grounds, paths, and around sidewalks, deck areas, and patio areas, emptying trash receptacles, cleaning out firepits and splitting wood for the guests). 
The tools used to complete the above tasks are weed trimmer or string trimmer, lawn mower, rakes, shovels, chainsaws, power pruners, backpack blowers, gloves and safety gear, hand shears and axes for splitting firewood for the guests. 
In addition, in response to the RFI, we would like to confirm that the specific tasks of the Groundskeeper position related to "putting in and taking out boat docks." can be clarified as follows: 
In the springtime, the Groundskeepers help with setting up the boat docks as the Petitioner has floating boat docks. They take them off the shoreline in the early spring by pushing them out on the water in the bay by boat and securing the floating boat dock sections to the posts set up in the lake. 
In the fall, the Groundskeepers unsecure the floating boat docks from the posts in the lake and float them over to the shoreline for winter storage. They keep the sections together and fasten them to stay in shallow water by the shoreline for the winter. 
The lifting and moving of the boat docks is completed using winches, jacks, or wheel kits to lift and maneuver the heavy sections.

When one side of the funnel cake is brown, the worker would use a large spoon (to drain grease) to flip the half-cooked funnel cake. When the funnel cake is fully cooked its placed into a tray to allow the excess grease to drain while keeping the funnel cake at a proper serving temperature. Same process is done for corn dogs.

The employer will be providing pizza, French Fries, funnel cake. Tools: Gas deep fryers; gas grills; food warmers, knives, tongs, potato cutter, can opener. The pizza is placed on the upper counter, under heat lamps and then a worker who would be working the front counter would then: Slide the pizza down to the lower counter. Using a Pizza Cutter the worker would cut the pizza and: 

Either place the whole pizza in a box, and deliver to the customer that ordered the custom pizza; or Place individual slices on paper plates, and then slide those plates into the heated display case. The worker would pour prepackaged frozen fries into a fryer (similar to what someone would see at any other fast food establishment), when the timer goes off the basket raises itself automatically to drip the hot grease back into the fryer, then the worker when the second buzzer sounds, would pick up the basket with gloved hand, dump onto a tray, sprinkle with salt, and place into the appropriate container (medium, large or family sized). Continual cleaning of the work area during the shift and at the end of the day. Puttin supplies into refrigeration – or discarding. Are part of the duties. The workers would stand in front of the specific unit they oversaw operating. On one side of the unit there would be the raw materials (funnel cake batter). A portion of the batter would be taken from the container mixed in and scooped using a large ladle into the funnel cake pourer. The batter would be poured slowly into the basket, in one general location, so that strings of batter would cling together. When one side of the funnel cake is brown, the worker would use a large spoon (to drain grease) to flip the half-cooked funnel cake. When the funnel cake is fully cooked its placed into a tray to allow the excess grease to drain while keeping the funnel cake at a proper serving temperature.

DECKHANDS WILL ATTEND THE MORNING SAFETY BRIEFING LED BY THE CAPTAIN OR DESIGNATED SAFETY OFFICER. THEY WILL REVIEW THE DAY’S PLAN, WEATHER CONDITIONS, AND SAFETY PROTOCOLS. THEY WILL INSPECT AND PREPARE ALL FISHING GEAR, NETS, TRAWL EQUIPMENT, AND DECK MACHINERY FOR USE AND ENSURE THEY ARE IN GOOD WORKING CONDITION. THEY WILL CLEAN THE DECK AREA TO MAINTAIN A SAFE AND ORGANIZED WORKSPACE. THEY WILL REMOVE DEBRIS, TRASH, AND ANY HAZARDS THAT MAY IMPEDE WORK. THEY WILL ASSIST IN DEPLOYING, RETRIEVING, AND MAINTAINING TRAWL NETS AND OTHER FISHING EQUIPMENT. THIS INCLUDES HANDLING HEAVY ROPES, CABLES, AND NETS SAFELY. THEY WILL SORT AND SEPARATE SHRIMP FROM THE BYCATCH. THEY HANDLE SHRIMP WITH EXTREME CARE TO MINIMIZE DAMAGE AND MAINTAIN PRODUCT QUALITY. THIS INCLUDES PROPERLY STOWING SHRIMP IN THE FREEZER. THEY ASSIST IN THE SORTING, CLEANING, AND PREPARATION OF SHRIMP FOR FREEZING OR FURTHER PROCESSING. THIS MAY INVOLVE REMOVING HEADS, SHELLS, AND OTHER UNDESIRABLE PARTS. THEY FOLLOW STRICT HYGIENE PROTOCOLS TO PREVENT CONTAMINATION AND MAINTAIN FOOD SAFETY STANDARDS. THIS INCLUDES WEARING APPROPRIATE PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT (PPE) AND CLEANING WORK AREAS REGULARLY. THEY MAINTAIN THE CLEANLINESS AND ORDERLINESS OF THE DECK AREA. PROPERLY DISPOSE OF WASTE, INCLUDING FISH WASTE AND BYCATCH. THEY PERFORM ROUTINE MAINTENANCE TASKS ON DECK EQUIPMENT AND MACHINERY. THIS MAY INVOLVE GREASING WINCHES, CHECKING CABLES, AND PERFORMING MINOR REPAIRS. THEY ASSIST IN REPAIRING FISHING GEAR AND NETS AS NEEDED, ENSURING THEY ARE IN GOOD WORKING CONDITION. THEY PARTICIPATE ACTIVELY IN SAFETY DRILLS AND TRAINING EXERCISES. BE PREPARED TO RESPOND TO VARIOUS EMERGENCIES, INCLUDING FIRE, ABANDON SHIP, AND MAN-OVERBOARD SITUATIONS. THEY MAINTAIN A VIGILANT LOOKOUT FOR OTHER VESSELS, OBSTACLES, AND HAZARDS IN THE VICINITY AND REPORT ANY POTENTIAL ISSUES TO THE OFFICER ON WATCH OR CAPTAIN. THEY OPERATE AND MAINTAIN DECK EQUIPMENT RELATED TO NAVIGATION, SUCH AS WINCHES, BOOMS, AND CRANES. THEY KEEP ACCURATE RECORDS OF FISHING ACTIVITIES, INCLUDING CATCH QUANTITIES, FISHING LOCATIONS, AND ANY NOTEWORTHY EVENTS DURING THE TRIP.

Detailed List of Food and Drink Items:

 • Ramen: Tonkotsu, shoyu, spicy creamy, and veggie ramen (assembled from pre-made broths and prepared ingredients).

 • Onigiri: Tuna mayo and other fillings (assembled from pre-cooked rice and pre-portioned ingredients).

 • Grab-and-Go Items: Steamed buns, shomai, packaged snacks, and pre-prepared items.

Detailed Description of Tasks Involved:

 • Ingredient Handling: Measuring powders, syrups, teas, and coffee portions; transferring cooked boba into warmers; handling pre-made broths; portioning rice and fillings; storing items following food-safety standards.

 • Drink Assembly: Using digital scales, milk frothers, shakers, blenders, hot-water dispensers, and an espresso machine to create standardized drinks. Tasks include steeping teas, mixing powders, pulling espresso shots, shaking drinks, sealing cups, and adding toppings.

 • Food Prep: Heating pre-made ramen broth; portioning noodles; adding toppings (egg, nori, tofu, vegetables); assembling onigiri with molds; wrapping with seaweed; steaming buns or shomai using a countertop steamer; placing items into containers or packages.

 • Tools/Equipment Used: Espresso machine, hot-water dispenser, digital scales, refrigeration units, blenders, shakers, ramen pots, steamers, rice cooker, sealing machine, portioning scoops, knives, cutting boards.

 • Made-from-Scratch vs Pre-Made: Teas, coffees, and fruit drinks are assembled in-house using powders, syrups, and brewed ingredients. Ramen broth, boba, steamed buns, shomai, and onigiri fillings are pre-made or pre-portioned, then heated and assembled by staff. No advanced cooking is required.

These individuals will utilize a large range of kitchen skills, including, slicing, grinding, frying, and baking during food service. This chef is also responsible for the detection and disposal of unattractive and spoiled foods. They will promote safety culture in all areas in the workplace, including food safety, customer safety, and the safety of team members. 

Essential Functions 
• Work with the executive chef to produce diversified menus in accordance with the restaurant’s policy and vision. 
• Conceptualize new dishes which appeal to clients. 
• Establish the working schedule and organize the work in the kitchen so that everything works like a “well-oiled machine”. 
• Produce high quality dishes that follow up the established menu and level up to location’s standards, as well as to clients’ requirements. 
• Plan the food design in order to create a perfect match between the dish’s aspect and its taste. • Discover talented staff and train them in order to reach the high standards of the location. 
• Train the auxiliary kitchen staff in order to provide best results in minimum time and using at the maximum the available resources. 
• Maintain order and discipline in the kitchen during working hours. 
• Create tasting menus for clients interested in contracting the food for different events (wedding, banquets, corporate dinners, birthday parties, etc.) from their kitchen. 
• Developing a cross-marketing strategy in order to increase profit, such as pairing drinks with the food served. 
• Make sure the hygiene and food safety standards are met in all stages of food preparation, starting with the ingredients and ending with the finished dish which leaves the kitchen door. 
• Make sure that the professional equipment is in good conditions and signal any malfunction before it affects the staff or the clients. 
• Routinely, prepares and stocks the food assembly line for service, and in addition, replenishes the service line, as needed. 
• Accurately assembles food in a timely manner, as ordered, during all meal services. 
• Observes company health, sanitation, and food quality policies. 
• Ability to communicate clearly (verbal and written) with employees and management. 
• Ability to prioritize work, meet deadlines, and maintain an attention to detail while working in a team or group environment.

***RFI Response***
The structural members are for construction and structure framework. Iron being worked could be for main rack, vendor steel, or perimeter structures around job blueprint.

Ironworkers can be involved in the operation of manual or hydraulic hoisting equipment's if training has been given or if a competent person (Rigger) is overseeing the rigging activity.

But as for rigging structural members for erection from a crane hook, Ironworkers do not perform that activity. Ironworkers can help clear travel paths or possibly hold a tag line. If the Ironworker is a connector, they might be involved in safely removing rigging hardware after steel member has been installed.

Responsibilities would be operating hoisting device as directed by (team) directions, typically being a qualified rigger with proper training. Tending with operation might/will include assisting with installing hoisting hardware. Etc. operating elevating work platform to work location or helping move hoisting equipment on location. Any craft member can operate a hoisting device if they have gone through the proper training, but would only be considered assisting and not directing the hoisting operation.

Ironworkers do not operate any crane or rigging when steel erection is being done with a crane. The rigging department handles that as a whole. Ironworkers can assist by moving steel with forklift assistance for crane setup or notifying the riggers what pieces will be installed & in which order. Ironworkers will also be able to tend as in clearing flight paths for steel erection or holding taglines. More likely than not riggers on a crane work in pairs so no rigging hardware will need to be handled by Ironworkers.

***Original F.a.2***
Prepare and construct structural members to receive and support its intended structural load. Sets up hoisting equipment for raising and placing structural-steel members. On most projects the crane and rigging department sets the cranes for steel erection. Install steel members into approximate position while member is supported by hoisting device. Install members into final position, using turnbuckles, sleaver bars, porta powers, and hand tools. Align holes in member with corresponding holes in previously placed member by using drift pins, bull pins, spud wrenches and sleaver bars. Verify vertical and horizontal alignment of members, using Bazooka bobs, lasers, and levels. Bolt members to keep them in position until they can be permanently bolted or welded in place. Modify steel members to make alterations, using oxyacetylene welding equipment or grinders with metal cutting discs. Assist in lifting, positioning, and securing of materials and structural members during installation. 	Perform minor maintenance or cleaning activities of tools and equipment.

2.	Various long and short handled tools (e.g. wrench., hammer).
3.	Power tools (e.g. magnetic drill, cutting torch, pneumatic impact wrench).

General duties: The Nanny is to provide responsible care for the Child(ren) and assistance to the household. This includes, but is not limited to, the following:

• Gentle Guidance: In no event will the Child be allowed to engage in any activity that is deemed dangerous by the Parents or the Nanny. The Child is not to be spanked, hit, or touched with force for transgressions.

• Transportation: The Child will not be transported without the Parents' prior consent and instruction. The Child will not be transported to someone else's home without prior consent of the Parents. All transportation will be with the parent's vehicle and with proper car seat provided and approved by parents. No person, other than parent-approved and daycare center finger-printed child care providers or babysitters, has permission to transport the Child without the Parents' prior consent. The Parents will cover all reasonable associated fees, including the Nanny's car insurance.

• Meals: The Nanny will arrange for full meals three times per day and healthy snacks at least twice per day.

• Naps: The Nanny will ensure that the Child follow the nap schedule provided by the Parents.

• Guidelines for Releasing Children: The Nanny will not release the Child to any relative, neighbor, family friend, or friend's parent without the Parents' direct authorization to do so.

• Time Outside: The Nanny will spend at least ninety (90) minutes each day outside with the Child, weather permitting in the backyard of parent homes or parent-approved parks. The Nanny will ensure adequate sun or rain/snow protection for the Child (e.g., sunscreen, jackets, etc. to be provided by the Parents).

• Regular Updates to Parents: The Nanny will provide the Parents with a daily update via text message, photo, video, or email (or call if necessary) at least once during each day. Cellphone costs will be covered by the Employer.

• No Guests: No guests are permitted without the Parents' prior consent.

• Emergency Protocol: In case of emergency, the Nanny will call 911 and/or the Parents immediately.

Domestic Duties: The Nanny will be responsible for the following domestic duties as part of her job:
i. Baby dishes/bottles: Daily cleaning of all bottles, cups, dishes, etc. that are used by the Child and the Nanny (e.g., the Nanny will put dirty dishes in the dishwasher, run the appropriate dishwasher). The Nanny will also vacuum and/or sweep under the kitchen tables and wipe down tables and countertops after eating, as needed.
ii. Play Area: Pick up toys and clothes daily in the Child's play areas (including Child's rooms, play room, living room, and outside).
iii. Other Domestic Duties: The Nanny will be responsible for certain other domestic duties as reasonably requested by the Parents (e.g., packing the Child for trips, organizing the Child's bedroom, etc.). The Nanny is also responsible for washing, drying, folding and putting away child's clothes.

Special Requirements: THE NANNY AGREES THAT SHE WILL OBTAIN CARDIOPULMONARY RESUSCITATION (CPR) AND FIRST AID CERTIFICATION TRAINING FOR INFANTS BEFORE OR AFTER ARRIVING TO THE WORKSITE. THE EMPLOYER WILL COMPENSATE THE NANNY FOR THE COSTS OF ANY SUCH CERTIFICATION.

***RFI Response***
Work environment: Bartenders will work in four (4) primary environments: the bar area, the lounge, the dining area, and service storage areas/service stations (where service supplies are stored/organized for use during service). Duties in these areas are outlined below. 
Restocking Service Areas: Bartenders restock glassware, garnishes, mixers, napkins, and bar tools at the bar service station. They may also restock non-alcoholic beverages and condiments in the server drink station as needed. Restocking includes retrieving supplies from the storage room, organizing items in designated service areas, and ensuring items are clean and set for service. 
Delivering Beverages to Guests: Bartenders will deliver drinks directly to guests seated in the dining room, lounge, or bar area. This task includes carrying beverages on a tray or by hand and confirming the correct order is delivered to the correct guest. 
Cleaning and resetting tables: Bartenders assist servers by clearing used glassware, plates, and utensils from tables in the lounge or bar seating area. They may wipe down tables, replace linens or coasters, reset glassware, and ensure the seating area is prepared for the next guest. 
Maintaining cleanliness of bar and lounge areas: Bartenders will wipe bar surfaces, clean spills immediately, organize bar tools, sweep the bar or lounge floor as needed, and ensure compliance with sanitation policies. Bartenders also polish glassware, maintain trash receptacles, and assist with end-of-shift cleaning. 
Assisting servers with guest requests: Bartenders may prepare specialty drinks requested by servers for their guests and support servers during high-volume service with tasks such as taking drink orders from customers at tables when servers are overwhelmed or unable to reach the bar in a timely manner, running drinks to tables when needed to maintain service pace, refilling guests beverages when servers are unavailable, running bar food or small plates to tables when bar-back or kitchen support is limited, wiping down bar surfaces and guest-facing areas to maintain cleanliness during service, and addressing immediate guest needs (such as providing water, napkins, or condiments). These duties are performed in the bar, lounge, and dining areas as needed. 

***Original F.a.2***
Bartenders will be responsible for mixing and serving alcoholic and non-alcoholic beverages to members and guests in all areas of the club, taking and placing guest orders, serving snacks and beverages, and assessing bar customers' needs and preferences to make drink recommendations. Additional duties include cleaning glasses and bar equipment, ensuring the bar and work areas are clean and well-stocked, counting bar and beverage inventory and maintaining required bar and beverage inventory records, and assisting with other front-of-house duties as may be requested by management such as restocking service areas, delivering beverages to guests, clearing and resetting tables, maintaining cleanliness of bar and lounge areas, assisting servers with guest requests, and ensuring overall guest satisfaction in the dining and bar areas.

Work hours: 35 hours per week.  The work schedule may vary and can include evening and weekend hours.  Work is performed on any day of the week from Monday through Sunday.  Days off vary. Shifts vary (example shifts: 9:00am - 4:00pm, 12:00pm - 7:00pm, or 3:00pm - 10:00pm).

See job description for additional information.

***RFI Response***
In order to extract, trim, and split granite blocks to be used for structural and
dimensional granite construction projects the employees must first drill either 10 20 or 30 feet
into the formation using a John Henry Deep Drill. Once a proper hole is drilled the employees 
must thread a wire with diamond grit beads through the hole and attach the wire as one 
continuous loop through the hole onto a Pelligrini Diamond Wire Quarry Saw. The employees 
then run this machine to cut through the formation. This process (drilling and cutting) is repeated
until a large wall of granite is separated from the formation. Once separated the employees then 
split (using smaller hand-held diamond bit drills followed by feather-and-wedge steel bits which 
they hammer) or wire-saw the wall in order to cut the wall into blocks (typically 9’ x 5’ x 5’ or 
larger if requested by customer). After the blocks are created the employees must move the 
blocks using a front-end loader modified with forks sufficient to lift 60,000 pound blocks of 
granite to a separate area on the quarry so that they may be selected by customers for purchase.
The depth and length of the wall and quantity and sizes of blocks cut from that wall are
conditioned by the cracks, natural rifts and seams, and aesthetic color and pattern of the natural 
granite formation. 

***Original Duties***
Labor to be performed includes to drill and saw into the granite formation and extract large blocks to be used for structural and dimensional granite construction projects; trimming blocks; splitting blocks.

Use irrigation methods to adjust the amount of water consumption and to prevent waste, such as many tools used to aid in the adjustment of water that is used for irrigation systems, including Rainbird adjustment tool, pressure gauges, irrigation controllers valves and water flow meters. The proper run times and frequency of these specific types of irrigation within one climate is performed for commercial and residential environments. Water lawns, trees, or plants, using portable sprinkler systems, hoses, or watering cans. Haul or spread topsoil, or spread straw over seeded soil to hold soil in place. Maintain or repair tools and equipment. Install and maintain irrigation systems, which include  two primary type of irrigation systems that are used for commercial and residential project environments: 

Sprinkler Irrigation - Water is pumped through a pipe system and then sprayed onto the landscape/lawn through rotating sprinkler heads. 

Drip Irrigation or trickle irrigation - water is conveyed under pressure through a pipe system to plants where it drips slowly onto the soil through emitters or drippers which are located close to the plants. Only the immediate root zone of each plant is wetted.

With these two methods we use a variety of equipment such as: shovels, rakes, walk-behind trenchers, pipe cutters or hacksaw, knives, measuring tapes, wire cutters and wrenches to install and maintain the irrigation systems.

DUTIES INCLUDE: WORKING DAILY IN THE PROCESSING PLANT. WORKERS WILL PREPARE AND PACKAGE ALL SHRIMPS AT THE PALMER FOODS FACILITY. THE SHRIMP IS RECEIVED FROZEN FROM THE FISHING VESSELS AND BROUGHT INTO THE FREEZER WHERE THEY ARE STORED. THERE ARE NO TOOLS REQUIRED WHILE RECEIVING THE SHRIMP FROM THE VESSELS. FIRST, WORKERS WILL THAW THE SHRIMP BY DUMPING IT INTO VATS THAT CAN HOLD UP TO 700 LBS OF SHRIMP. THE ONLY TOOL USED FOR THE THAWING PROCESS IS A PLASTIC SAFETY UTILITY KNIFE, WHICH IS USED TO CUT THE SHRIMP SACKS OPEN. THE VATS ARE THEN FILLED WITH WATER AND STORED IN THE REFRIGERATOR AT 32°F. AFTER 24 HOURS, THE SHRIMPS WILL BE READY FOR THE GRADING PROCESS. GRADING PROCESS IS WHERE THE SHRIMPS ARE SORTED BASED ON SIZE. DURING THE GRADING PROCESS, WORKERS WILL USE MANUAL PALLET JACKS TO MOVE THE VATS AROUND AND PLASTIC SHOVELS TO KEEP ICE ON THE SHRIMP AT ALL TIMES. ONCE THE SHRIMP IS GRADED, IT IS THEN TRANSFERRED DIRECTLY TO THE IQF (INDIVIDUAL QUICK FREEZE) TUNNEL. THE IQF TUNNEL FREEZES THE SHRIMP BEFORE THEY ARE PACKAGED. ONCE THE SHRIMP MAKES IT THROUGH THE IQF LINE, IT IS THEN PACKAGED IN BOXES AND STORED IN THE FREEZER UNTIL IT IS SOLD TO A BUYER. THERE ARE NO TOOLS REQUIRED DURING THE IQF PROCESS. THIS PROCESS ONLY REQUIRES LIGHT MANUAL LABOR AND ALL WORKERS MUST WEAR PROPER PPE PROVIDED BY THE COMPANY. LIGHT MANUAL LABOR INCLUDES PICKING OUT ANY SHRIMP WITH DEFICIENCIES, MONITORING SHRIMP ENTERING THE MACHINE, PUTTING SHRIMP INTO BOXES, PLACING STICKERS ON THE BOXES AND PLACING THE BOXES ON A PALLET TO BE STORED IN THE FREEZER. WORKERS WILL ALSO KEEP THE FACILITY CLEAN.

Our landscape laborers perform the following duties.  In the early part of the season, they perform clean-up work with rakes, chain saws, tarps, and shovels; they spread mulch using shovels, rakes, wheel barrows, and blowers.  When the weather is warm enough, they plant trees, shrubs, and flowers and water them in using shovels, picks, rakes, wheel barrows, hoses, and watering cans.  Then, when the grass starts to grow, they mow and trim grass using mowers, line trimmers, edgers, and blowers.  Throughout the growing season, they will lay sod, haul topsoil, smooth out uneven terrain, trim trees and bushes, clean up leaves and other debris, fertilize lawns, and pull weeds, using rakes, wheel barrows, rototillers, pruners, shovels, and other hand tools.  As the season comes to a close, the mowing slows down and the work shifts to Fall clean ups which consists mostly of raking leaves and other debris.  These duties are performed for both our residential and commercial clients.

JOB DUTIES: All driver job duties are compensable and are common to the commercial truck driving industry. Drivers are to only accept dispatch orders that come directly from the employer and must contact the employer immediately after both the loading and unloading of each order. Drivers are to notify the employer prior to fueling their tractor-trailer at a retail fuel station. Drivers are to notify the employer prior to their personal usage of the tractor-trailer. Drivers will have no assistant. Drivers will have no passengers other than as directed by the employer. As is common in the industry, drivers will have great discretion regarding daily time management as the driver operates within the daily time parameters of their job duties directed by the employer, thus, not all hours that a driver is in their truck and off-worksite is compensable to the driver. The employer will provide the driver with means of securing their tractor-trailer while off-site and the driver must secure their loaded or unloaded tractor-trailer prior to and during the time when the driver is not performing job duties, therefore, the driver is not responsible for the tractor-trailer or the loaded product when not performing job duties. Compensable job duties include driving a tractor-trailer semi-truck from product pickup locations directly to delivery locations, including engaged-to-wait, timely and safely on public and interstate highways while adhering to all applicable laws and regulations pertaining to commercial motor vehicles; maneuver tractor-trailer into loading and unloading positions; hook and unhook trailer(s) to reposition loaded and unloaded trailers; verify the security, safety and proper care of the tractor-trailer and of the loaded product to include any written or verbal product care instructions that may be required; dispense fuel into the tractor-trailer at both the worksite and retail fuel stations; complete and document pre- and post-trip safety inspections of the tractor-trailer as required by law (49 CFR §396.11); perform minor maintenance and safety duties for the tractor-trailer (example, add fluids, change windshield wipers, etc.) as necessary, as is common to the industry; timely and accurately document (example, time and activity) and submit to employer all work related activity via paper and electronic methods; real-time data entry into employer provided Electronic Logging Device (ELD) as required by law (Section 32301(B) of the Commercial Motor Vehicle Safety Enhancement Act, enacted as part of the MAP-21 Act) as well as maintain a commercial driver daily paper logbook, real-time data entry and document uploads into employer provided Transportation Management Software (TMS) to document order status and other order related issues, utilize the employer provided Global Positioning System (GPS) to successfully navigate to locations, real-time data entry and document uploads into employer provided digital payments system (RELAY) and other real-time data exchange apps that the employer may choose to utilize; all other various job duties common to the industry as directed by the employer.

The Associate performs the following specific food preparation tasks using delivered pre-cooked, pre-sliced, pre-washed ingredients and standardized, high-speed methods suitable for fast casual dining: 1. Hot Sandwiches and Paninis: • Menu Items: Chipotle Chicken Avocado Melt, Toasted Frontega Chicken, Turkey Bacon Bravo, Grilled Cheese, and breakfast sandwiches. • Method of Preparation: The Associate views a digital monitor (Kitchen Display System) for the order. They retrieve pre-sliced breads (e.g., Focaccia, Ciabatta) and assemble the sandwich by stacking pre-cooked/pre-sliced meats and cheeses and applying pre-made sauces from calibrated squeeze bottles. The assembled item is placed in a high-speed contact oven (TurboChef) where the associate presses a pre programmed button to toast the item for a fixed duration (approx. 45-60 seconds). Once toasted, they add pre-sliced and washed cold produce (lettuce, tomato) and wrap the item. 2. Salads and Cold Bowls: • Menu Items: Strawberry Poppyseed Salad, Green Goddess Cobb, Fuji Apple Salad, Caesar Salad. • Method of Preparation: The Associate uses specific color-coded scoops to measure pre-washed lettuce blends into a bowl. They add pre-prepped toppings (e.g., pre cooked diced chicken, grape tomatoes, feta crumbles) using portion-control utensils. They measure the specific dressing into the bowl, toss with tongs to coat, and transfer to a serving container. 3. Soups, Mac & Cheese, and Warm Grain Bowls: • Menu Items: Broccoli Cheddar Soup, Creamy Tomato Soup, Bistro French Onion, Mac & Cheese. • Method of Preparation: These items arrive at the facility fully cooked in vacuum sealed bags. The Associate performs "thermalizing" duties by placing the sealed bags in a hot water bath to bring them to serving temperature. They then pour the contents into heated wells on the service line. Upon order, the associate simply ladles the designated portion into a cup or bowl and adds a garnish (e.g., croutons). 4. Beverages and Smoothies: • Menu Items: Iced Green Tea, Lemonade, Espresso drinks, Frozen Fruit Smoothies. • Method of Preparation: The Associate operates automated brewing equipment for coffee and tea. For smoothies, they place pre-portioned fruit puree and ice into a blender and press a pre-set cycle button. For espresso drinks, they utilize an automated machine that grinds and pulls shots automatically, requiring the associate only to steam milk and pour.

Prepare and cook traditional Salvadoran recipes using artisanal methods.

Maintain cleanliness and food safety standards throughout all food handling and preparation processes.

Maintain consistency in taste, presentation, and quality of all traditional products.

Assist in inventory control of ingredients used in traditional recipes.

Job Summary:
The Landscape Groundskeeper is responsible for maintaining the aesthetic and functional aspects of various properties' landscapes, gardens, and grounds. This role includes planting, mowing, watering, fertilizing, pruning, and overall landscape maintenance to ensure the grounds remain attractive, healthy, and well-groomed.

Beneficiaries' key responsibilities include mowing, edging, and trimming lawns and using hand or power tools. Plant, water, fertilize, weed, and mulch flower beds, trees, shrubs, and lawns. Prune and trim trees, shrubs, and hedges to maintain desired shapes and sizes. Remove debris, leaves, and litter from the grounds and dispose of them appropriately. Maintain pathways, driveways, and walkways, ensuring they are clean and safe.

Equipment Operation and Maintenance:
Beneficiaries will maintain and operate landscaping equipment such as lawnmowers, trimmers, edgers, leaf blowers, and other tools. They will perform routine maintenance to ensure they are in good working condition.

Irrigation and Watering:
Beneficiaries set up, adjust, and monitor irrigation systems to ensure proper watering schedules and coverage. They also manually water plants and lawns as needed.

Seasonal Maintenance:
Beneficiaries will perform seasonal planting and preparation, including aerating, seeding, and fertilizing lawns.

Safety and Compliance:
Beneficiaries will adhere to all safety guidelines and procedures while performing tasks and ensure compliance with local, state, and federal landscaping and grounds maintenance regulations.

Physical Requirements:
Beneficiaries must be able to lift, push, pull, and carry heavy objects (at least 50 pounds), stand, walk, perform repetitive tasks for extended periods, and work outdoors in varying weather conditions.

**RFI**
Lemonade: On a cutting board, the lemons would be cut in half. See through plastic cups and lids would be brought from storage. A food service quality bin of ice with ice scoop would be placed into the work area. Workers serving lemonade would stand at the front counter, communicating with the customer. After taking the order, scoop ice into a cup, pick up (with gloved hand), and squeeze Lemon Juice into the cup, using a Juicer. Pre-mixed sugar water would be poured into the cup, and then the product would be served with lid and straw to the patron after payment. 

Corn Dogs: We purchase our corn dogs frozen, pre-battered and preskewered. The Corn Dogs are removed from the freezer and allowed to thaw. Once thawed out they are placed into the deep fryer. The timer for the deep fryer that is reserved for the corn dogs is pre-set, and when they are in the fryer for the proper amount of time the basket raises to allow for the oil to drain from the corn dogs. They are placed into a chafing dish on the front counter under a heat lamp. When a client orders one or more corn dogs, they are removed from the chafing dish and placed into the proper size serving boat. They are handed to the client who would go to the kiosk to add condiments and pick up any napkins or cutlery required. 

Cotton Candy: Cotton Candy is a combination of sugar and a commercially available product known as flossine, that adds color and flavoring to the finished product. A commercial grade cotton candy a/k/a floss machine is turned on, allowed to heat up, the sugar and flossine is mixed in appropriate amounts, and when the machine hits the correct temperature, the worker slowly pours from a small cup or measuring scoop, the mix into the center /heating element. This produces whisps of cotton candy that forms around the center of the machine. The worker learns how to spin the cotton candy cone within the large bowl, to capture and build what most persons would recognize as cotton candy. This is then slipped into a cotton candy bag and sealed either with a small tie and placed into a dry location for a few hours of storage until a client orders a cotton candy.

JOB DUTIES: All driver job duties are compensable and are common to the commercial truck driving industry. Drivers are to only accept dispatch orders that come directly from the employer and must contact the employer immediately after both the loading and unloading of each order. Drivers are to notify the employer prior to fueling their tractor-trailer at a retail fuel station. Drivers are to notify the employer prior to their personal usage of the tractor-trailer. Drivers will have no assistant. Drivers will have no passengers other than as directed by the employer. As is common in the industry, drivers will have great discretion regarding daily time management as the driver operates within the daily time parameters of their job duties directed by the employer, thus, not all hours that a driver is in their truck and off-worksite is compensable to the driver. The employer will provide the driver with means of securing their tractor-trailer while off-site and the driver must secure their loaded or unloaded tractor-trailer prior to and during the time when the driver is not performing job duties, therefore, the driver is not responsible for the tractor-trailer or the loaded product when not performing job duties. Compensable job duties include driving a tractor-trailer semi-truck from product pickup locations directly to delivery locations, including engaged-to-wait, timely and safely on public and interstate highways while adhering to all applicable laws and regulations pertaining to commercial motor vehicles; maneuver tractor-trailer into loading and unloading positions; hook and unhook trailer(s) to reposition loaded and unloaded trailers; verify the security, safety and proper care of the tractor-trailer and of the loaded product to include any written or verbal product care instructions that may be required; dispense fuel into the tractor-trailer at both the worksite and retail fuel stations; complete and document pre- and post-trip safety inspections of the tractor-trailer as required by law (49 CFR §396.11); perform minor maintenance and safety duties for the tractor-trailer (example, add fluids, change windshield wipers, etc.) as necessary, as is common to the industry; timely and accurately document (example, time and activity) and submit to employer all work related activity via paper and electronic methods; real-time data entry into employer provided Electronic Logging Device (ELD) as required by law (Section 32301(B) of the Commercial Motor Vehicle Safety Enhancement Act, enacted as part of the MAP-21 Act) as well as maintain a commercial driver daily paper logbook, real-time data entry and document uploads into employer provided Transportation Management Software (TMS) to document order status and other order related issues, utilize the employer provided Global Positioning System (GPS) to successfully navigate to locations, real-time data entry and document uploads into employer provided digital payments system (RELAY) and other real-time data exchange apps that the employer may choose to utilize; all other various job duties common to the industry as directed by the employer.

A helper roofer, also known as a roofing assistant or roofing laborer, plays a crucial role in supporting the roofing team with various tasks related to roof installation, repair, and maintenance. The specific duties include:

Material Preparation:
Assist in loading and unloading roofing materials, tools, and equipment.
Organize and transport materials to the work area.
Set up and dismantle scaffolding and ladders as needed.

Tool Assistance:
Hand tools and equipment to roofers as required.
Keep tools and equipment clean and in good condition.
Learn to properly use and handle basic roofing tools.

Site Cleanup:
Collect and dispose of debris and waste materials.
Keep the work area clean and organized.
Sweep and clean the roof surface as needed.

Safety Compliance:
Follow safety guidelines and procedures at all times.
Wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE).
Report any safety concerns or incidents to the supervisor.

Material Handling:
Assist in the installation of roofing materials, such as shingles, tiles, or metal sheets.
Hand materials to roofers and help with positioning and securing them.

Team Collaboration:
Work closely with other team members to ensure smooth workflow.
Communicate effectively with lead roofers and supervisors.

Basic Repairs:
Learn to identify and address minor issues with roofs.
Assist in basic repair tasks under supervision.

Communication:
Follow instructions from supervisors and communicate effectively within the team.
Report any issues or concerns promptly.

It's important to note that the role of a helper roofer serves as an entry-level position

Roofing helpers use a variety of tools to assist with the installation, repair, and maintenance of roofs. These include

Utility Knife: Used for cutting roofing materials, such as shingles or felt.
Hammer: A basic tool for driving nails during the installation of roofing materials.
Nail Gun: Speeds up the process of fastening nails and is commonly used for larger roofing projects.
Tape Measure: Essential for measuring and marking roofing materials to ensure accurate installations.
Chalk Line: Used to create straight reference lines on the roof surface for alignment purposes.
Pry Bar or Crowbar: Helps with the removal of old roofing materials during repair or replacement.
Roofing Nails: Common fasteners used to secure roofing materials.
Roofing Shovel or Roof Ripper: Used for removing old roofing materials, such as shingles or tiles.
Roofing Hatchet: A specialized hammer with a hatchet blade for cutting and trimming roofing materials.
Roofing Square: A framing square designed for roofing applications, assisting in making accurate measurements and cuts.
Roofing Gloves: Provide hand protection and a good grip when handling materials.
Safety Harness and Lanyard: Essential for working at heights, ensuring the safety of the roofing helper.
Caulking Gun: Used to apply sealants and adhesives for waterproofing.
Screwdrivers and Wrenches: Basic hand tools for assembling and disassembling roofing components.
Roofing Adhesives and Sealants: Used to bond and seal various roofing materials to prevent leaks.
Safety Glasses and Hearing Protection: Personal protective equipment (PPE) to ensure safety on the job.
Ladders: Required for accessing different areas of the roof during installation or repair.
Roof Brackets and Scaffolding: Used to create a stable working platform when working on steeper roofs.

It's important for roofing helpers to be familiar with the proper use and maintenance of these tools, as well as to follow safety guidelines to prevent accidents and injuries on the job.

Mobile amusement rides are designed to be assembled and disassembled in 
hours with manual labor and simple tools, such as screwdrivers, pliers, 
rubber mallets, a variety of wrenches and ratchets.
When heavy lifting is required, rides would have built in hydraulics for 
raising and lowering platforms, etc., so that additional equipment is not 
required.
Once ride is positioned by a supervisor, workers would unload by hand, the 
various parts of the ride that must be assembled. Platforms and 
centerpieces would be positioned using the built-in hydraulic lifts and 
winches. Support bars and passenger conveyances would be positioned, 
and attached as per manufacturer instructions. 
Safety fence and signage, would be downloaded by hand and placed into 
position by hand. 
Popcorn: The popcorn machine is fixed on the counter. Worker would 
switch it on, scoop popcorn oil out of the container, using a measuring cup 
and pour it into the heating element. Worker would then scoop popcorn 
kernels out of the container, using a measuring cup and pour the kernels into 
he heating element. Worker would then scoop popcorn flavoring “Flavacol” 
from the container, using a measuring scoop and sprinkle this on the kernels. 
Worker would close the lid on the heating element, the popcorn would start 
popping, the lid would open due to the force of the popcorn, and the popped 
popcorn would fall into the bin. Worker would sprinkle the popcorn with 
additional flavoring, which would be in a shaker (like a saltshaker). Scoop 
the popcorn into individual popcorn boxes and place them onto the counter 
under a heat lamp. Another worker, when the popcorn was ordered by a 
patron, would take a container and then (depending upon what the patron 
requested) use the pumper to pump melted butter and/or shakers to sprinkle 
additional flavoring(s) onto the popcorn and then hand to the patron.
Fountain Drinks: When an order comes in for a certain flavor of drink, 
size, ice/light ice/no ice, from a patron, the worker would prepare the proper 
size, flavor drink and place a plastic lid on the cup. Worker would place the 
drink into the serving area so that another worker, with direct contact with 
the patron, is able to provide the drink(s) to the patron, along with the 
balance of the order.

Workers assist with jobsite setup by unloading and staging shingles, underlayment, tools, and safety equipment; placing tarps and protective coverings; and setting up ladders, ladder stabilizers, roof brackets, and fall-protection systems. Workers help with tear-off by removing existing shingles, tiles, metal panels, underlayment, flashings, vents, and other components using roofing tear-off shovels, pry bars, nail pullers, hammers, and utility knives. Workers gather debris into dumpsters or containers and keep work areas clean using brooms, shovels, and magnetic nail sweepers.

Workers assist with roof deck preparation by helping remove damaged sheathing, handing materials to experienced roofers, and installing replacement decking as directed. Workers help apply underlayment, ice-and-water barrier, and synthetic moisture protection by rolling out materials, cutting them to size with utility knives, aligning them to chalk lines, and fastening with cap nails or nail guns under supervision.

During installation of shingles, tiles, or metal roofing systems, workers carry bundles or panels to the roof, stage materials, hand shingles, fasteners, and components to roofers, and assist with layout of starter strips and courses. Workers use tape measures, chalk lines, roofing nail guns, hammers, screw guns, and impact drivers as directed to support correct exposure, straight lines, and required fastening patterns per manufacturer specifications.

Workers also assist with installation of flashings, drip edge, step flashing, vents, ridge vents, and pipe boots by cutting materials, handing components to roofers, and applying roofing cement or sealant with trowels or caulk guns as instructed. For low-slope systems, workers may help roll out membrane sheets, apply adhesive, press seams with hand rollers, and secure edge metal under supervision.

Workers use standard roofing tools including roofing shovels, pry bars, hammers, utility knives, screw guns, impact drivers, chalk lines, tape measures, magnetic sweepers, trowels, caulk guns, and leaf blowers. Workers also use ladders, ladder hoists, scaffolding, roof brackets, and OSHA-compliant fall-protection equipment such as harnesses, lifelines, and lanyards, following all safety practices.

Responsible for physical labor and concrete work at construction sites; work to grade or sub-grade specifications; use screeds; place concrete; load and unload forms and stakes; set forms; and complete other tasks as assigned. Engaging in concrete work includes working with Curb and Gutter, Sidewalks, Driveways, Concrete Pavement, and Decorative Concrete at construction sites. Placing concrete includes spreading the ready mixed concrete that is being discharged from a concrete mixer with a shovel or come along tools to the height of the forms. Screeding concrete is the process of leveling out the concrete to the height of the forms that are set in place. Setting forms is the process of placing wooden or steel forms in place to the elevation and alignment required for the concrete that will be placed inside the forms. Other tasks assigned include carrying forms and stakes and laying them out to get ready to be set into place, fine grading the subgrade with a shovel or come along, removing forms after the concrete has been placed and is set up, spraying curing compound onto the new concrete surface, and cleaning tools that have been used during the day.

Our landscape laborers perform the following duties.  In the early part of the season, they perform clean-up work with rakes, chain saws, tarps, and shovels; they spread mulch using shovels, rakes, wheel barrows, and blowers.  When the weather is warm enough, they plant trees, shrubs, and flowers and water them in using shovels, picks, rakes, wheel barrows, hoses, and watering cans.  Then, when the grass starts to grow, they mow and trim grass using mowers, line trimmers, edgers, and blowers.  Throughout the growing season, they will lay sod, haul topsoil, smooth out uneven terrain, trim trees and bushes, clean up leaves and other debris, fertilize lawns, and pull weeds, using rakes, wheel barrows, rototillers, pruners, shovels, and other hand tools.  As the season comes to a close, the mowing slows down and the work shifts to Fall clean ups which consists mostly of raking leaves and other debris.  These duties are performed for both our residential and commercial clients.

Funnel Cakes aka Elephant Ears: Gold Medal Products1 sells the mix that is used. Following instructions printed on the mix container, pour the mix plus appropriate liquid into the commercial batter blender. Once the batter has been blended, a portion is scooped into a dispenser bottle. The deep fryer (Empura EGF-45/55 LP Propane 15.5# Double Basket Commercial Gas Frier with 50-pound capacity, 120,000 BTU) would contain cooking oil at the proper temp. Worker would slowly pour batter from dispenser bottle into the fryer so that strings of batter would cling together, forming a funnel cake or elephant ear (same thing, different names). When one side of the funnel cake is brown, the worker would use a large slotted spoon (to drain grease) to flip the half cooked funnel cake. When the funnel cake is fully cooked the worker would use the same large, slotted spoon to pick the funnel cake from the fryer, hold it for a few seconds to drain, and place into a chafing dish under heat lamp to allow the excess grease to drain while keeping the funnel cake at a proper serving temperature. After customer places order, worker would use tongs to place funnel cake into serving boat and add selected topping such as sugar, shipped cream, compote, chocolate sauce and/or Nutella to the funnel cake and then provide the product to the customer. 

Fountain Soda. These workers would NOT be serving any alcoholic beverages. For a fountain drink, the worker would take the proper size cup in their hand, use an ice scoop to scoop ice into the cup (unless the patron ordered no ice), place the cup under the nozzle, press the cup against the lever and fill the cup. Take a lid from the lid supply and secure this on the top of the cup. From time to time, the worker would need to restock the cups, lids and ice from the appropriate storage area. If the machine runs out of syrup, the worker would need to locate in the storage area the proper flavor of prepackaged syrup, disconnect the empty syrup container, connect the full syrup container, and then discard the empty container.

PERFORMING MANUAL LABOR THAT WILL DEVELOP & PROTECT FORESTED AREAS DESIGNATED FOR HARVESTING COMMERCIAL TIMBER IN THE FUTURE.
COMMERCIAL TREE PLANTING WILL BE CONDUCTED ON STEEP TERRAIN; 40% - 50% IN SLOPE AND DONE WITH THE USE OF A PLANTING SHOVEL. THE EMPLOYEE WILL CLEAR A SPACE ON THE GROUND, DIG A HOLE, CORRECTLY PLACE THE SEEDLING TREE IN THE HOLE, ENSURING THE SEEDLING DOES NOT HAVE A "J" ROOT, COVER THE ROOTS WITH THE SOIL, AND THEN TAMP THE SOIL
AROUND THE NEWLY PLANTED SEEDLING. SPRAYING AND TUBING AS REQUIRED. THE EMPLOYEE WILL BE REQUIRED TO CARRY A PLANTING BAG WHICH WILL CONTAIN THE SEEDLINGS, COMBINED WEIGHT OF 50 - 60 POUNDS, DEPENDING ON THE SIZE OF THE SEEDLINGS, UP AND DOWN THE STEEP TERRAIN IN REMOTE MOUNTAINOUS AREAS. ACCESS IS GAINED TO THESE REMOTE AREAS BY TRAVELING ON ABANDONED, UNNAMED OR NUMBERED, LOGGING ROADS THAT ARE NOT MAINTAINED.
CUTTING OF UNDESIRABLE & DISEASED TREES. BRUSH CUTTING/PILING AND THE CUTTING OF LIMBS OF THE COMMERCIAL TIMBER WILL BE CONDUCTED IN ORDER TO ENCOURAGE THE GROWTH OF THE TIMBER. WORK WILL BE CONDUCTED IN AREAS THAT WERE LOGGED OFF 12-15 YEARS OR SO AGO AND THE UNWANTED GROWTH WILL BE ELIMINATED IN ORDER FOR THE STANDING TIMBER TO BE ABLE TO MATURE AT A GREATER RATE. WHEN REQUIRED, SELECTED LIMBS OF THE REMAINING TREES WILL BE CUT.  DURING THE BRUSH CUTTING THE EMPLOYEE WILL GATHER FOREST MATERIAL ACCUMULATED ON THE FOREST FLOOR (DUFF) AND PILE ALONG WITH THE CUTTINGS.
ALL OF THE CUTTING, TREES AND BRUSH, WILL BE PERFORMED WITH THE USE OF A CHAINSAW. EMPLOYEES ARE EXPECTED TO MAINTAIN THE SAW, SHARPEN THE CHAIN IN THE FIELD, MAINTAIN THE CORRECT FLUID LEVELS, AND PROPERLY BREAK THE SAW DOWN FOR CLEANING. THE COMBINED WEIGHT OF THE SAW, FUEL, AND OIL IS APPROXIMATELY 30 POUNDS AND MUST BE CARRIED UP AND DOWN DIFFICULT TERRAIN RANGING FROM 40% - 50% IN SLOPE. THE EMPLOYEE MUST DEMONSTRATE THAT HE CAN WORK IN A SAFE AND EFFECTIVE MANNER

***RFI Response Fiscal Year 2024***
Although the employer may hire more than one worker in this position, the employer intends the specific job duties/functions of the position to be able to be performed by a single person. More specifically, the position involves a combination of the duties of a housekeeper (O*NET Code: 37-2012), server (O*NET Code: 35-3031), and front desk clerk (O*NET Code: 43-4081). Due to the small size of the Norumbega Inn’s staff and budget, the employer needs a worker filling this position to be able to multi-task such that all the position’s duties/functions will be performed by a single worker in the position at various times during a given day or workweek. It is not fiscally feasible for the employer to hire separate workers to perform each of the tasks associated with the different occupational codes.
 
To further clarify, the varying duties of this position correspond to the three different occupational categories as follows:

Job Summary
The Electrical Laborer will perform manual labor tasks to assist electricians in electrical conduit installation, cable pulling, and related electrical infrastructure work under the direction of licensed electricians or supervisors at construction sites.

Essential Job Duties
Site Preparation and Material Handling: The worker will load, unload, and transport tools, conduit, cable reels, and electrical materials to and from work sites. The worker will prepare work areas by clearing debris and ensuring safe access. The worker will set up ladders and scaffolding as directed under direct supervision. The worker will stage materials at designated locations, organize jobsite storage, and assist in unloading delivery trucks.

Conduit Installation Support: Under direct supervision, the worker will measure, cut, and assist in bending electrical conduit, including EMT, PVC, and flexible conduit, per layout specifications. The worker will assist with installing conduit runs overhead, underground, and within walls, following the electrician's instructions. The worker will assist electricians with installing and securing conduit using straps, hangers, supports, and fasteners per NEC requirements. The worker will assist with installing conduit, threading conduit sections, and coupling conduit sections. The worker will assist with installing junction boxes, pull boxes, and conduit fittings.

Cable Pulling and Installation: The worker will assist in pulling electrical cables through conduit using pull ropes, fish tape, lubricant, and mechanical equipment. The worker will feed cable into conduit openings and maintain proper tension. The worker will assist electricians in labeling and routing cables per the electrical plans and the supervisor's direction. The worker will coil and store excess cable and assist in measuring and cutting cable to specified lengths.

Equipment and Tool Operation: The worker will operate hand tools, including hammers, wrenches, pliers, screwdrivers, tape measures, and levels. The worker will operate power tools such as drills, saws, conduit benders, threading machines, and cable pullers. The worker will perform routine tool maintenance, report damaged equipment immediately, and clean and organize tools daily.

Support for Licensed Electricians: The worker will assist electricians with layout, measurements, and component installation. The worker will deliver materials and tools to work areas, hold materials during installation, and assist with cleanup.

Safety Compliance and Housekeeping: The worker will follow jobsite safety rules, OSHA regulations, and company policies. The worker will properly use required PPE including hard hats, safety glasses, gloves, and steel-toed boots. The worker will follow lockout/tagout procedures and immediately report hazards or unsafe conditions. The worker will maintain clean, organized work areas and properly dispose of waste materials.

Documentation and Communication: The worker will record material usage, cable lengths, and installation progress. The worker will complete daily time sheets and work logs accurately. The worker will communicate effectively with supervisors and coworkers regarding tasks and job site conditions. The worker will report work progress and participate in daily safety meetings.

Job requires worker to prepare trawler for fishing activities; put nets into water and retrieve them; sort and head shrimp catch; return undesirable and illegal catch to sea; place shrimp on baskets, rinse, dip in preservatives; assist rigger to ice down shrimp in boat's hold, get nets back into water to resume trawling, and retrieve them again; offload catch, mop deck, assist captain and rigger as required.  Worker must have no less than one continuous month of experience as shrimp boat deckhand/header on a commercial, ocean-going 
shrimp trawler.  
Usual work period is from 11:00 pm (night) to 7:00 am (morning) except immediately following the opening of Texas waters on/around July 15th and short periods where there may be an abundance of catch.  Working conditions may include excessive heat and/or cold, rainy days and cold weather on deck, confined spaces, sleep deprivation, and fatigue.  
Worker is required to carry and repetitively lift an average of 75 pounds of headed shrimp to/from boat’s hold.  Work will be performed at sea for up to 60 days or more days aboard commercial shrimp trawlers in the Gulf of Mexico's Exclusive Zone (EEZ), depending on the amount of catch and/or expansion of fishing grounds.

Hotel, Motel, and Resort Desk Clerks will be responsible for greeting, registering, and assigning rooms to guests of hotels or motels, making and confirming reservations, and keeping records of room availability and guests' accounts, manually or using computers.  Duties include:
•	Contact housekeeping or maintenance staff when guests report problems.
•	Issue room keys and escort instructions to bellhops.
•	Verify customers' credit, and establish how the customer will pay for the accommodation.
•	Post charges, such as those for rooms, food, liquor, or telephone calls, to ledgers, manually or by using computers.
•	Review accounts and charges with guests during the check-out process.
•	Record guest comments or complaints, referring customers to managers as necessary.
•	Compute bills, collect payments, and make change for guests.
•	Transmit and receive messages, using telephones or telephone switchboards.
•	Answer inquiries pertaining to hotel services, guest registration, and travel directions, or make recommendations regarding shopping, dining, or entertainment.
•	Advise housekeeping staff when rooms have been vacated and are ready for cleaning.
•	Perform bookkeeping activities, such as balancing accounts and conducting nightly audits.
•	Clean and maintain lobby and common areas, such as restocking supplies and watering plants.
•	Prepare for basic food service, such as setting up continental breakfast or coffee and tea supplies.
•	Date-stamp, sort, and rack incoming mail and messages.
•	Arrange tours, taxis, or restaurant reservations for customers.
•	All other duties as assigned.

Measure distance from reference points and mark guidelines to lay out work, using plumb bobs and levels. Specific Refreshments to be prepared and served to patrons and their method of preparation are as follows:
Soft Serve Ice Cream. Tix is poured into a Soft Serve Ice Cream Machine, connected to electricity and water, and we get the ice cream.
The toppings are in small bins at the front edge of the counter. Scoop the toppings onto the ice cream. Mix the ice cream using whatever ingredients the customer wants, using a spatula. The spatula is also used to scrape the mixed ice cream off the counter and into a bowl or waffle cone for serving.
Corn Dogs: Corn dogs frozen, pre-battered and pre-skewered. Placed into the deep fryer. The timer for the deep fryer is pre-set, and when they are in the fryer for the proper amount of time the basket raises to allow for the oil to drain from the corn dogs. They are placed into a chafing dish on the front counter under a heat lamp. When a client orders one or more corn dogs, they are removed from the chafing dish and placed into the proper size serving boat. Workers serving lemonade would stand at the front counter, communicating with the customer. After taking the order, would scoop ice into a cup, pick up (with gloved hand), and squeeze Lemon Juice into the cup, using a Squeezer. Pre-mixed sugar water would be poured into the cup, and then the product would be served with lid and straw to the patron.

Installation of rock gardens, ponds, decks, drainage/irrigation systems, retaining walls, fences, planters, and/or playground equipment: When these tasks arise, the workers’ role is strictly limited to assisting senior staff with simple, manual labor such as placing small rocks, positioning pre‑fabricated edging, setting lightweight planters, holding materials, digging shallow holes, leveling soil, spreading mulch or gravel, and assembling pre‑made components. All structural construction, heavy equipment operation, cutting, framing, or specialty system installation is performed by supervisors or subcontracted specialists.

2. Grading and contouring for proper drainage: Workers assist with light soil preparation only. Tasks may include raking soil, smoothing surface areas, filling low spots by hand, lightly tamping loose soil, and following supervisor instructions to create minor surface contours. Materials consist of soil, sand, mulch, and small aggregate. Tools are limited to rakes, shovels, hand tampers, and wheelbarrows. Workers do not perform machine grading or operate grading equipment.

3. Installation of pavers: Workers assist with basic site preparation and placement of individual pavers by hand. Tasks may include removing debris, leveling bedding sand with a rake, aligning pavers, and lightly tapping them into place with a small hand mallet. Materials include sand, gravel, and pre‑manufactured pavers. Tools include hand trowels, rakes, levels, knee pads, and rubber mallets. Workers do not cut pavers, operate saws, or perform masonry.
